# Command Resolution and Bounded Leverage

## Command philosophy

Commands are intents, not reality edits.

They resolve against:
- autonomy
- institutional constraints
- relationship pressure
- available resources
- risk
- world state

## Good player leverage

- money
- grain and material support
- prestige
- favors and obligations
- clan authority
- office leverage when present
- force resources when present
- public legitimacy, rumor control, and visible moral credit when present
- information reach through letters, runners, markets, temples, schools, clerks, or military routes when present
- coalition pressure across households, lineages, yamen, merchants, soldiers, temples, and local elites when earned

## Bad player leverage

- direct stat edits
- guaranteed obedience
- instant world rewrites
- bypassing module ownership
- direct "change history" buttons
- free court control without access, faction, procedure, risk, or legitimacy
- rebellion, usurpation, or dynasty founding without supply, force, public claim, administrative capacity, and backlash

## Good command windows

- family policy and support allocation
- branch favor or mediation
- education funding
- route investment or withdrawal
- limited office review
- limited campaign posture and support
- public relief, guarantees, petition backing, rumor-facing gestures, or temple/market sponsorship
- coalition building when the player has enough influence footprint
- high-level political pressure only after the player has office, faction, force, wealth, or legitimacy pathways into it

## Design checks

- what is the player actually deciding
- what friction stands in the way
- what module owns the resolution
- what immediate receipt appears
- what delayed consequence appears next month
- what resistance, reinterpretation, or opportunistic use by adults and institutions may occur
- what legitimacy, favor, fear, debt, shame, or grudge is created by the attempt
